Hi, welcome back to module six of Straight Line Persuasion. Now, this is where it really, really gets good. We're going to have an introduction to the actual tactics of the straight line. And this is where you're going to learn literally how you take a sale and make it goal oriented, how you take an influencing, a negotiation, whatever it might be. And know before you even start what your outcome is and have a very clear define how to get from here where you start to here, which is where you need to be. You're going to learn a couple of very powerful things. You're going to learn number one, how to get, take that ability now with tonality to gain rapport, how you actually use that rapport now to gather intelligence, how to ask very specific questions, starting with big picture questions, then slowly getting more specific to really identify what your prospects core needs are. See, once you know someone's needs, then you can really develop a strategy to address those needs versus being the hard charging pit bull salesman that just wants to close, close, close, close, without having the empathy for what the client really wants. So you're going to learn something called the three tens. Now, these are the three things that have to line up in a moment in time, not forever, in one moment of time to get someone to buy. When you have the three tens in place, I'll tell you what they are right now. Number one, that they absolutely love your product. Number two, they absolutely trust and connect with you. Number three, that they trust and connect with your company. When those three elements line up in a single moment in time and you ask for the order, believe me, you will see magic happen. See, most of the time, what an influencer doesn't do is they leave one of those things out. And because of that, they keep banging their head against the wall, wondering why they're not getting the appropriate result or the preferred result when actually just simply left a very key element out of the equation. Now, sometimes you'll also find, you'll see this in the DVD, that you might have the three tens lined up, but there'll still be something holding the client back, the prospect back, from taking decisive action. That has to do with something called limiting buying beliefs. And what those are, is they're unconscious wounds, so to speak, that exist within all human beings, including myself, including you. And these wounds actually give us that voice in the back of our heads, don't take action, don't buy, and it might be something their mom told them when they were this big, or it might be some bad experience they had in the past with another salesperson, something they read in the paper, saw on TV. And those limiting buying beliefs are these killers of closing. So what you're going to learn to do here, as you learn the basics of straight line technology, is to actually create that massive rapport instantly in the first few seconds, to use that rapport, to gather intelligence that will help you then tailor your presentation, and also tailor your offering to really suit your client's needs. Because ultimately, remember, what we're trying to do here is get people what they want, not just close a sale, we're looking to get people what they want, and most importantly, what they need. So you're going to learn how to create the three tens, elegantly and effectively, and you're going to learn to get them into one distinct moment of time. This sort of spot where people are just saying, wow, they know they should buy, they want to buy, and if there's a buying belief holding them back, you can learn to squash that using some very powerful language patterns. So this is it. This is the point, really, where it all starts to come together, where you're going to learn to take the earlier lessons of prospecting, tonality, and you're going to apply some structure to it to actually take the client from A to Z in a step-by-step, move-by-move formula. And remember, keep that action book out, take notes. If you have any questions, you can always press the Rewind button and look at something again. And remember, anything in the past, any situations where you couldn't influence somebody, you couldn't persuade, those things are going to melt away once you really master the art of straight-line technology. So go for it. Keep your eye on the video, DVD, and I'll see you soon. From this point forward, it's all tactics of how you actually move down the straight line and the language patterns and so forth and how you get to the close. But I'll give you a really brief background of how the straight line came to be. And what happened was, is I had first, I heard a couple of things simultaneously. First was the actual straight line itself was the ability to take a sale and break it down to a very logical path along the straight line. Just take this for what it's worth right now. Just imagine a sale being a straight line. When you open up the sale, the second the word comes out of your mouth, you're here. This is your open, and the close is right here. When you get the person's information and they say they're buying, that's the close. The shortest distance between any two points is what? A straight line. This is your perfect positioning from start to finish. Now we all know that in the real world, it doesn't go this way. So we have what we call boundaries, healthy boundaries that go off the straight line. And they're very powerful, they're helpful. You have a boundary above the line, and you have a boundary below the line. And it's between these boundaries, and I mentioned this before, where you develop rapport and gather intelligence. And you're going to do that in a process of qualifying questions. So you're going to have questions that you ask the people that are very specific. They start off as, write this down, big picture questions. And then slowly you whittle down to specific questions. You're looking to gather massive amounts of intelligence from this person in a way that's not annoying, in a way that builds rapport, in a way that empowers them. You ask a question one way, I ask it another way, we get completely different answers from the person, and completely different emotional responses. If I'm looking to qualify a person for money, let's say in the stock market for instance, and I want to say, how much are you trading with right now? I could say to someone, how much are you trading with right now? He'll say none of your damn business. How much are you trading with right now? You know, Inspector General, how much are you trading with right now? People don't want that. Say, how much are you trading with right now? By the way, get a curiosity, how much are you trading with right now? Totally different response you will get by asking the questions in an offhanded manner. Totally different. Now what I was talking to him before, I was mentioning about people sending me, I get emails all the time, two or three a day about people that are using the straight line. These are extra-tonites people that worked for me and they got scripts and they somehow took part of the system and they're using it. They're doing great. They're always really nice warm letters that change my life. I swear by the straight line. If you go to my Facebook wall, you see some of these funny postings there out there about the straight line. They trade scripts around. You'll see portions of scripts on the wall. It's really funny. Anyway, invariably, when I get someone that really wants to talk to me about the straight line, they'll start sending me more and more stuff. Can you help me? What do you think of this? They'll send me a thing saying, after the first closes and the person doesn't buy, they'll ask a question. Like saying, well, let me ask you a question. They'll say, money aside, does the idea make sense to you? Forget about, let's say you're talking about credit repair. Say, you're trying to sell someone on credit repair. You say, well, money aside, let's say it's $900. I'm just making up a number. And they say, no, not really. So you say, well, money aside, does the idea of credit repair make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Good or bad? All wrong. Money's not a side. There's money involved. When you say money aside and you go forward and you're done, you've done yourself a huge service. Now the guy will invest this whole thing in it. And he's saying, money aside, money aside, he's off the line. He's not in the sale. Now you can say money aside without saying it. So I got this script the other day from some kids saying, and the first rebuttal was, money aside, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Which could also be money aside, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Same or different? What I just said, first and second. Same or different? Different. Right? Tonality is completely different. Money aside, I say to you, money aside, money aside, let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Money aside. We're not friends here. We're adversaries because I know you don't want to pay. We're not on the same page here. That's the tonality when you're talking like that. So I say to you, well, let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like it? You said, no, fine. Well, let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? I don't get it. Another tonality. That's also wrong. If you want to take somebody who said, no, when whatever, balked at the price, okay, and the first thing, and I'm jumping forward here for a second, I'll get back to the front. But if you're going to ask somebody, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Implicit in your tone is that money is set aside. You don't have to say it. If I say to you, and I say, pick up, you know, try out for the program, it's $1,000. Fair enough, the guy says, well, let me think about it. So I say, money aside, let me ask you a question. Wrong. If he says, let me think about it, I say, well, let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? What did I just say? Money aside. Without ever saying, of course, you know, I'm saying, well, let me ask you a question. Like, money aside. I pushed the money aside with my tone of voice. That's the secret to closing. As soon as you say money aside, you're separated from the guy. Okay, you start laying out these beautiful pictures, like this bastard, he's laying out all these benefits and, you know, you see money aside, I know the money's coming back in. He's not doing this for academic exercise. You lose credibility. You're like, you know, let me push money off. He's like, what a stupid cheap sales trick that is. They see through that in a second. Never say the word money aside. We live in fantasy land of the real world. The real world, money is here. We need it. You can't get this. Okay. There's other ways to make it more affordable. You do step down, sell some of the mechanisms that we have. You would go to a lower program. But money's never not in the picture here because it is in the picture. But with tonality, we disarm somebody. You say, well, let me ask you a question. You know, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? He's like, well, yeah. He knows what you meant, but you never said it. So you stay in rapport. That's jumping heads. So here we are. Okay. In the first portion of the sale, we're asking him all these questions. I'm going to go through all these questions one by one tomorrow. And I'm going to show you the precise tonality you need to ask each question in to get the most out of it. And to make them like you, trust you, and to realize what three things. Let's see. What are they? What are they? Sharpest attack. It never changes. It's so simple. You can see when you start training sales, you can see it's so simple. Okay. There's three things you're always trying to accomplish. Number one, your sharpest attack. Two, your enthusiastic as hell. Three, you're a force of nature. You're a force of, a tour of the force, a force of record, an expert. These three things, they're always lurking. You're always moving them as you move towards the line. This never, ever ends. On the other side of the equation, what do you need to accomplish? You know before you open up the, before you pick up the phone, before you open the door, face to face, before you bang on it, whatever it might be, you know you need to accomplish these three things. Number one, he's got to love your product. You know this. Take it out of the equation. There's no shortcuts. He's got to love your product. He's got to trust and love you. Like you know you care. He's got to love and trust you. And he's got to love, trust your company. You know this going in. The constants. Okay. Your company. Every tonality is precisely engineered. I don't mean that in a deceptive way of talking in a way to empower people to get the maximum amount of intelligence when you ask them a question so you respond gracefully. I say to somebody, how much money do you have in the market? That's pretty invasive. Feel screwed. You know I'm not telling you how much I have in the market. You don't earn the right to ask me that question. Or say, how much money do you have in the market right now? Just the ballpark. Which one's better? Which one works? Which one gets you the response? The second one. All the time. You got the first question to ask them? No. You wouldn't ask them money questions first. You're going to ask them other questions before to get them into the habit of saying yes. And giving information. Once you're in further reports say, oh, by the way, what do you have in the market? How much do you owe on the mortgage right now? Or how much are you behind right now? Just the ballpark. Get it? How much money? How much do you owe on the credit cards right now? Whatever it might be. Okay. Triggers are response and always, always, always as you move towards the line, these three things must stay intact and you're establishing these things. At a certain point in time, what's going to happen is when these three things are established, firmly established, I call them the three tens. We're going to go through this in intimate detail. The three tens. We mean someone cannot be at a six. Tending. He thinks it's the best thing since sliced bread. He hates it. He's going to be a 10 for sliced bread. 10 for you. 10 for your company. And bam. Then you've got a shot to close. One of those is lower. You're not going to close. You get into this spot by moving him down the line and you're strengthening your case. You're strengthening your case. You're strengthening this case logically and emotionally. Not just emotionally. Wipe out that crap you might have ever read that people don't buy on logic. It is not true. You need to develop an airtight logical case of why your product is the best thing since sliced bread, why it fills his needs, why he should buy now logically. Will he buy now? Not enough. They also need the emotional component. But without logic, you're not going anywhere fast. That being said, we pick up the phone. We start speaking to someone. I say just assume for a second as I go through the concept of using all the right tonality. You're getting into instant rapport with somebody. You're gathering intelligence. You're asking them questions. One of the classic mistakes that I always see happening at this point is someone will think that developing rapport is talking about the guy's life. That's not what developing rapport is about. So if you're on the phone, I think about two months ago, was it actually in England when I was trading this company in England? And I was listening in on someone's phone call and they were qualifying to buy a trading system, a currency trading system. And he asked this guy, what kind of work are you doing right now? He says, oh, I'm like a macarbon or whatever. I was living in the northern England somewhere. Great. And he goes, how much time do you want to spend trading? You want to be a... He didn't sound this good. He sounded awful. He was dead. How much time do you want to spend trading? He goes, well, I just want to trade part time because my son's in the military and he's up stationed up there in northern Britain and he's flying fighter planes right now. And the sale goes, really? I love flying fighter planes. It's so cool. And I listened for 15 minutes as they talked about freaking fighter planes in northern England. And that somehow turned into deer hunting in northern England. And they were talking about everything but the sale. The salesman thought he was getting reported this person. Untrue. Okay. Let me actually logically, logically, would an expert spend his time with a stranger talking about fighter planes in England on a phone call? Only a desperate salesman, desperate for a commission and desperate to talk to anybody who will talk to him. Well, we'll go on that train and that's not how you get respect from somebody. Okay. What happens is as you ask your qualifying questions, you're moving down the line and you're moving down the line and you're moving down the line. Forget how far I'm moving right now. Okay. I'm going to move far because I want to show you a visual. You're moving down the line. You're asking questions. You're looking for information to find that if he's qualified, meaning if he could afford your product and he should buy your product. It's in his best interest. If he shouldn't buy, what will you do? Either aid directly into a place where he can get what he wants. If you can't do that, he's hang up the call very nicely, have a nice day. You're not going to pressure this person into buying. If he shouldn't buy or can't afford to buy it. Especially if he can't afford to buy because you're wasting your time because you're going to bang your head saying, why isn't he buying? He can't afford it. Okay. As you're moving down the line, if the person's not giving you the intelligence that you need and he tries to go off the line far, far, far, okay? You get to here. This is as far as you will let the sale go off the line. You're going to stop him and you're going to redirect him back to the line and get him back into the sale. You're not going to go off into Pluto. You're going to control the sale. Now you cannot interrupt somebody while they're talking. That's bad. So how do you stop? It's very simple. You wait until they're done with their little speech they give you and you very quickly say, oh my God, that's so great. By the way, boom, how much money you have in the market right now? It's really simple. But here's the trick. It's tonality that carries the day. He starts rambling. You're like, I don't care about that shit in the countryside. How much you have in the market? He'll say, what an asshole. The salesman doesn't like me, care about my needs. But if someone said to me, talking about the stuff in the countryside, I'm saying, oh, wow, that's really great. No, I know. I love the countryside. By the way, you say you're looking three hours a day, right? He'll say, yeah, he's back on the line. It's that simple. This is not complicated. Let me tell you something. I believe simple is good. Complicating is interesting. It doesn't work. Simple works. That's why anyone can embrace the system here. Simple works. This is really, really simple. All you're going to do is you're going to stop him and use very powerful tonality. I'll show you what it is when we get to it, to carry the day, to bring him back to the line, to get the intelligence and move forward. Every time the guy tries to go off the line, he's going to go, he's going to go, he's going to go, you're going to stop him, turn him back to the sale, using tonality to carry the day. And with your tonality, implicit in your tonality, write this down. He knows number one that you care. He has to know that you care. Be that you're interested in his life. That you are interested. Not that I don't give a crap about that, but time is money. And since I am sharp as a tack, enthusiastic as hell, and a force to recommend an expert, honestly, I love to talk. It has to be closed. You have one day we can go off the drinks. Not now. Not when I'm qualifying you. People will say, well, Jordan, you know, people just ramble. How do you stop that? I'm like, they don't ramble when they speak to me because they know better. Because they know what? It's all back to the same crap because they know I'm sharp as a tack, enthusiastic, I'm an expert. You get that? And when was that established? In the first four seconds and reinforced every step of the way. That first paragraph, right? They're like, ooh, this guy's on the, they're like, they don't know it. They're like, he's on the money. They got a salesman who's on the money. You ever have a salesman who's on the money? Raise your hand. What do you do? You play his game because you know you got a pro. I have a woman in Beverly Hills, I get my BMW's from. Her name is Neda. She's the top salesman in the country for BMW. This woman is so sharp and so directed. And I think she loves me and all she's doing is selling me a car on like four minutes flat. It's somehow in four minutes flat, she's like loving me, she's liking me, filling my needs, is great, how's the family, blah, blah, blah, blah, signing the dialogue, I got myself a car. You don't need to ramble. You don't need to, you know, be this person that like has to ramble with someone to be an effective salesperson. I knew that Neda was an authority figure, the top person, sharp as a tack, her time is valuable. I respect that, not just because I'm a salesperson. I respect that any human being will respect that if you command that respect. And it comes through in what? Your toe, say it. Tonality, okay, it comes back in person with your body language and how you carry yourself. Okay, so that's the bottom line. So here we are, we're moving down the line. Now, okay, we go through this whole thing, we qualify the person, we gather all the intelligence that we need. We've been massive rapport with this person. They like us. Maybe there's a laugh or two. They know we care. They don't know why. It's almost unconscious. They're happy to be on the call. You brighten their otherwise un-cheery day, right? You enter most people's world. Remember, most people at any given moment are in a disempowered state. Most people live in disempowered states. When you come in with a positive state, you brighten their day. If you come in in a disempowering, who needs this guy? I got enough misery in my life without the salesperson. When someone comes in, boom, cheerful, upbeat, how you doing? Like, okay, up a little bit. Just that much is all you need to get in the door to prove that you're those three things. I'll tell you a story. I was getting health insurance, life insurance. This is a great story. Before I left for Australia, the last time I was going, it's like seven, eight months ago, and I made the fatal mistake of trying to get it over the internet. I felt that this whole application, which generated a phone call from some woman who was the driest human being on the planet. She was 20, 21 years old by the sound of her, and she started asking me qualifying questions for life insurance. She starts off, your name, your social security number, what day, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, right? Now, starts the real question. She's like, what do you do for a living? I said, well, I'm an author and a public speaker. Okay, check that. She goes, have you ever flown any planes before, aircraft? I'm like, yeah, actually, I'm helicopters. Haven't flown in 10 years, though. Just helicopters, 10 years, haven't flown. She goes, so that's a yes? I said, yeah. Now, remember, it's the driest tone in the world. Just is that a yes? I'm like, yes. She goes, okay, have you ever flown any fixed wing aircraft? I'm like, no, no, just helicopters. She goes, so the answer is no. She goes, no. Have you ever flown a crop duster? I'm like, no, I never flown only a helicopter. She goes, no crop duster, no. Have you ever flown for the news, reporting for the news? I'm like, what are you talking about? I said, now, I'm in a rush, by the way. It was the day before I was leaving and I'm starting to get pissed. I'm supposed to be a master of state management. I'm losing my state and I'm getting pissed off, right? Then she goes, have you ever flown for the military? I'm like, no, I flew a helicopter for pleasure. I pushed her up the side of my yard because I was high on a drug. You happy? That's it. I get a bit of silence. She goes, so the answer is no. I'm like, no. She goes, have you ever flown? No. She goes, have you ever flown? No. She goes, mister, unless you asked, I can hear the full question. I cannot take a no. I'm like, this bitch, I'm going to kill this woman. I'm like, stop pacing around now and I'm really pissed. I start getting into a really pissed off state. And she goes on and on, asking for spy planes, military planes, agriculture. I never knew there was so many planes. I can't tell you, it was 50 types of planes. And finally, I'm like, listen lady, I'm going to, and I saw a curse. I'm going to fucking rush. I am pissed off. This is bullshit. I got to go somewhere. She's like, hold on for a second. I'm like, she pissed me on hold. I'm like, oh my God, she's going to hang up on me now. I'm going to have to start this crap again. All right? And so I'm so pissed, okay? She comes back. She goes, okay, I'm done with those questions. She goes, next question, have you ever used drugs? I'm like, oh my God. I'm like, drugs, mountains of them, cocaine, quailudes, massive quies, heroin, Xanax, clottop, you name it. I'm like, dead silence. I can hear the fear coming through the other edge of the phone, right? So now she's like, okay, let me just take these one at a time, right? She goes, have you ever snored a cup of, yes, five pounds of it. You happy? Dead silence, okay? Oh, hold on, sir. I'm like, oh my God, I'm on hold again. Now my girlfriend comes in. I'm like, this is you. I'm looking for blame, right? You put me up to this. You made me get life insurance, blah, blah, blah. I'm just like yelling at her. She's like, what's wrong? She goes, calm down. You're losing your sleep. I'm like, screw this. I want to be pissed off, right? I'm really angry. 30 seconds later, the girl gets back on the phone, all right? She's like, okay, sir, have you ever smoked marijuana? 50 pounds worth, you happy? I said, let me tell you something, bitch. I said, I am in a scope down or where you are. This is bullsh**. All of a sudden, I bring the woman to tears over the phone, okay? And she's crying. She's bowling on the phone. And all of a sudden, I'm like, oh my God. I'm like, look what I did. I'm in an angry, pissed off state. What did I do to this other person? I made them cry. My state affected someone else's state. It made someone cry, all right? I'm like, I'm Jesus. I'm supposed to be teaching this. That was on a tour. That was like a self-development tour. I'm like, oh my God, all right? So I said, stop. And I said, listen, this, I'm so sorry. I said, I'm so sorry. I said, listen, I'm just leaving somewhere. I'm just really uptight. It's not your fault. I said, listen, let me tell you a little bit about myself. And I started telling home my story. And three minutes later, she's laughing hysterically. I'm like, let's just go through this together. And I fill out the whole application, right? And so on and so forth. The point is this. When you're in an angry, pissed off state, you hurt everybody around you. When you're in a positive, pumped up state, enthusiastic, sharp, you make everyone around you go up to another level. As a salesperson, when you pop that first pop out of your mouth and the way you carry yourself on the phone, when I say carry, I mean on the phone, the way you carry yourself and the mental image you create about yourself to the other person rules the day. And if you're in person, same thing with body language. It's hugely important. So when people say to me, well, you know, they people ramble because it's you. That's just your story that they old people ramble. That's not a truth. People that are disempowered, they blame, blame, blame, blame, blame. They justify, justify, justify, justify. They complain, they complain, they complain. And remember what you do when you complain? What are you doing? You're focusing on all the crap in your life and you become a crap magnet. They say, why is my life so crappy? Because you're focusing on the crap. It expands and it comes to you. So simple. Yet so few people do the right thing and focus on the positive, the solution. So my point is this. When you pick up that phone and I Hey, John, how's it going today? There's a reason why you're looking to raise up the other person's state into one of happiness, positive. They don't know why they're happy to speak to you. Does it work all the time? No. No. But we live in a game of percentages, right? Closing X percent. Each little notch you make that raises you up. It starts to build and build the number. Before you know it, it's a snowball effect. Plus you feel a lot better about yourself. Every moment of the day, if you're going out there raising people's states versus trying to knock them down and rip their eyeballs out, it's a different way you live and look to yourself. OK, so you go through all these questions and you ask them like 10 to 15 questions. It's fine. The more intelligence, the better. You're gathering, identifying needs like that old, like the new sales model that says it's all about. Yes, you're doing all that, but you're saying it's closing. It's all done to move towards the close. So after you're done with that, then you give the presentation. You give your first presentation. OK, there's a transition. OK, and here is your transition. And it's, again, really, really simple. Because this is all really, really simple. The transition is after you've asked your last qualified question, you're going to say this no matter what. Unless it's not true. But if they fall into the category where they should be buying a product, you're going to say, well, let me say this. Based on everything that you just told me, this is the perfect fit for you. Boom, and you're into your presentation. That's it. That's the language pattern. It's so simple. Don't change it. It works. Works all the time. Not 99% of the time. If people say no, they were never interested. It works. That's your transition to the main body of the script. Well, let me say this. Based on everything you've just told me, this seems like a perfect fit for you. And what you're doing is you're giving your presentation, moving him down the line. Right? You've asked him questions. Bop, bop, bop. Right? You've gone off the line a bit. You've gone up. You've gone down. But the whole way, you've moved forward on the line. Right? And you get to the point and you say, OK, let me tell you how easy it is to get started. It's a question of your name, some basic information, credit card number, the product that arrives in three days. And believe me, you won't be sorry. Sound fair enough? Obviously, you wouldn't say it like that. But that's where you are in the sale. Where are you on the line? Anyone want to raise their hand? Anyone take a guess? Where are you on the line? Who thinks I'm here? Raise your hand. Anybody? Who thinks I'm here? OK, here. OK. Good. Who thinks I'm here? OK. Who thinks I'm here? OK. Who thinks I'm here? OK. They're right. All this, believe it or not, and the reason I move forward is just to show you a visual. That's why I said it's not really that. All of that, that first no, let the games begin. That's when the sale starts. When they say no. You must reframe it as a salesperson. The sales will begin until they say no the first time. Any monkey can read off a script to someone who's in that ultra high 20% of buyers. It's just so committed to buy, they're going to buy instantly. Well, what do you pay a sales like that? Minimum wage, it's called. They stand in the checkout line at 7-Eleven, and they do their salespeople too. I'll sell you a donut. Here, 35 cents. Here you go. Thank you. No pressure. You want to sell like that, transactional selling, then do that, $7 an hour, maybe even less in the city. That's selling, but influencing, real selling, persuading, whatever you want to call it, doesn't work that way. It starts when they say no. Once you figure that out, everything changes. Because you realize, OK, and let's say you're over here, OK, and you say, believe me, you will not be sorry. Sound fair enough? They say, yeah, great. Let me think about it. Not interested. Let me talk to my wife. I'm busy. Can you call back? Not a good time right now. But the truth is, you want to know the best way to answer an objection? Answer before they ask it. Anticipate it. I'm going to show you how to do that. In a straight line, you'll be anticipating all objections in looping and knocking out their objections before they even bring them up. Secondly, you're going to be knocking out their buying beliefs that limit them before they bring them up. He's never going to tell you, I'm not buying because my mommy told me not to buy from strangers. That's what holds most people back in truth, not to trust salespeople, not to do business with those people that we don't know, all that stuff. You're going to knock all that stuff out. He's not going to tell you, well, I'm running a mental calculation right now. And I sense that if I make this decision, there's a good chance I might feel more pain in the future than pleasure. He's not going to say that. But you know that's the truth. You know it's the truth. Knowing that empowers you. So as you move down the line, you're going to knock that out with a language pattern that addresses that very thing. Ultimately it gets to the point where you're going on and on down the line. And what's happening is very simple. Like I said before, he's got the positives here. He's got the negatives here. And you chip away as you move down the line, you're pouring on positives. You're pouring on positives. Pouring on positives, sometimes it's knocking out negatives. It gets lighter. Knocking out negatives. And then all of a sudden you crack a belief. And the scale drops. The scale doesn't drop like this. It's not like this. OK, oh boy. It's like no, no, no. All right, boom. When someone hits the action threshold, they buy like that. Who knows that as a salesperson? People say no, no, no, no. And all of a sudden you'll say one thing. All right, do it. That's how it works in sales. They're running a calculation. Positives, negatives. And there's this belief that acts as sort of this ultimate gravity force. The gravity is based on the beliefs. You keep adding on the positives, the benefits, logical benefits, emotional benefits. Scale gets heavier, heavier. Not moving yet. You start knocking away things that are holding them back, objections. Still not moving. All of a sudden that one thing, it's either logic or emotion, it could be anything. The scale drops and they buy. What is it? Is it logic? Is it emotion? Is it a belief? Guess what? Who cares? Using the straight line, I will simply loop and loop and loop and loop around the line until I hit it by accident if I have to. You're going to go after all of them. You never know. Human beings are complicated organisms, right? Getting someone to buy that doesn't want to buy to influence someone that doesn't want to be influenced, to persuade someone that doesn't want to be persuaded, right? Imagine their mind as a very high-tech combination lock. Three turns to the left to 32. Four turns to the right at 16. I think I got it backwards, but it doesn't really matter. Click one thing opens. Click, click, and all of a sudden you're going to click, and the safe opens and they buy. What you're doing as you go down the straight line is you're picking the lock for what their buying strategy is. Everybody has a buying strategy and it's different. What makes me buy something is different than you. It's just different. We're similar creatures, but we have our unique elements to us based on our beliefs that are formed since we're this big. From the times our mommy spoon-fed us apples, my mommy told me that the only noble way to wealth was to become a doctor. Guess what? I almost went to med school. I actually went to dental school. I went to dental school not because I wanted to be a dentist because my mom inserted a belief. Righteously, it means to help me that the only noble way to wealth is to be a physician. I was like, well, screw that. I'm not going to 13 years of medical school. That's crazy between residency and specialty, right? I said, well, if I'm a dentist, let's still be Dr. Belford. She'll be happy. I'll be happy. And dentists get rich. That's how my mind worked. I rationalized the whole thing out. Not verbally, but I really did. When I look back at it, that's what happened. So I go to dental school and I walk in. There's 105 dental students on the first day of orientation, and they're all sitting there looking, all chipper everything, and an auditorium. The dean is standing up on a stage like this. He's got gray hair. He looks very dental, a guy with a white jacket. He's like a dentist, very official. And he starts talking. He's like, welcome you all to the Baltimore College of Dental Surgery. You should be very happy. It's a great career you've chosen. You've worked hard to get it. You're going to be a pillar of the community and blah, blah, blah. I'm like, OK, great. And I'm looking around. Everyone's admiring it, expectantly. And then he goes, let me say this. The golden age of dentistry is over. If you're here to make a lot of money, you're probably in the wrong place. I'm like, what the fuck? I'm in the wrong place. I'm like, oh my god. And I got up on the first day and I walked out. Never came back to dental school. I dropped the first day dental school dropout. Now, of course, I couldn't bear to tell my mother that. So I hid down in Maryland for like four months. She called me out of school. Oh, it's really good. I'm doing great. I was working out all day, running, jogging, going out at night. And then the money ran out because I made a lot of money as a kid selling ice cream. I made a lot of money selling ice cream on the beach. I always had a lot of money hustling, selling ices. Blanket to blanket to blanket. Chipwiches, fudgicles, Italian ices. Made a lot of money and sustained myself through the year. When the money ran out, I came back up north to my house. And I sat down with my parents. They said, I want to be a salesman. They're like, what is the slimyest thing on earth? That's what my parents were. I love them to death. And they're really wealthy now. Thanks because when I made money, my dad worked for me. And I made them very wealthy. They live a very nice life. Two bedroom apartment I grew up with. It's like the wonder years of my house. I go back to the furniture in the same 1960s. It's unbelievable, my parents. Salt of the earth people. They live very frugally, but very modestly. And they're great and they're healthy. They're great people. When I sat down, I was like, they're like, a salesman. I'm like, a meat salesman. I'm like, oh my god, call the police meat salesman. Oh my god, my son, what happened, right? Anyway, the point I'm trying to make is that the beliefs that we hold drive us to the ends of the earth to be consistent with who we think we are, our identity, our identity. It's the highest level of conscience. We have our beliefs and our identity. Our identity is based on the beliefs of who we think we are, who we are. How we go about the world, move through the world, is based on the beliefs of what we think we can accomplish. We won't try things. We don't think we'll succeed at. So if I want to get someone to say, I knock out a belief, you get that? So you're just trying to sell someone. You better be really aware of what their beliefs are about buying. Where do they fit in? Rest assured this, 90% of the people that you go to try to sell have been told at one point or another, not to trust a salesman, or have been burnt by a salesman by getting bad service, by being overcharged, and thought themselves a fool or called a fool correctly so by someone that they knew and loved, and they felt crappy about it, and there's a wound there. In order for you to close, we have to overcome that wound. How do you overcome that wound? Very simple. You create, you create the following things. Number one, an airtight logical case. Number one, you create an airtight logical case of why it makes sense to buy when? Now, an airtight logical case, which means the product must be perceived as the best thing since slice bread. Number one, you must get them to this point. If you get them to this point, you've gone far. It's not always easy, especially when you're front-loading your scripts. If you go out and get a script, a sales script, and it's all about the great stuff in the first couple of minutes of the script, you have a problem. Because what happens is that you've shot your best load out there, the cannon, boom, right? You shot your cannon off with your best stuff, and they say no, having nothing to do with your product at all. There's other reasons why they're saying no, but you've got no powder left. Your best powder is not there. Here's the goal. Write this down. Leave your powder dry. Do not give them all the great stuff about your product all up front, because once they say no based on that, they have to remain consistent with the no. Better, you should leave some stuff out, the really great stuff, so when they say no, you're like, oh, you said no, but now let me tell you something else. We'll switch that. You get it? You can't say all this to them, but they know it. People are smart. Logical creatures and more emotional creatures. Both must be in place. So you must create an airtight logical case. Secondly, you must create an airtight emotional case. Those two things must be in place. Third, you must crack through all their limiting buying beliefs. Crack limiting beliefs. These three things will occur as you move down the straight line with specific language patterns and specific language patterns and scripts. Scripts, scripts and scripts, and they're all going to be ethical and honest. Do not lie in your scripts. Do not exaggerate in your scripts. You don't have to. You just don't have to. If you're selling a crappy product, you should quit the company you're working for and find a better place to sell. Your product's not the best. Go find someplace else where it is the best. As a salesperson, as a great salesperson, you deserve to be selling the best product, the best of breed. I know most of you are in that position and that's great. But if one of you is not, you just need to seriously rethink your career if you're selling garbage and crap. Because the straight line will work great for that stuff. Oh, it works really, really great. And you'll lose your soul and you might end up in jail. To rip people off when all the complaints come in because everyone that shouldn't have bought. And your company goes out of business and you're embarrassed. It's terrible. But if you have a great product and you have an ethical script and the script, I'm saying the script is crucial. The script is crucial because the script, the right set of scripts will allow you to pick the lock for any person's buying strategy. That's all you're doing. As you're running down the line, you're going to be picking the lock on their buying strategy. And here's the beauty. There must be like how many different buying strategies. I don't really know. I never thought about it, but I bet there's a lot. So what are we going to do? Are we going to try to identify what their buying strategies and figure it out and take a whirl? I want to tell you, if you're a training salesman, you're a sales person and you want to be effective, what would you rather have? Would you rather pick up the phone and know that every single sale is going to be the same? Every time. It's going to be the same way every time. That or would you like to try to free form it every single time and make it different? Which is better? The first, raise your hand if you want to do it the same every time. Raise your hand. That's the way. Who wants to free form to try to figure this crap out? That is a disaster. A great sales person, the best person can free form it. They can. They can go free form and they just know it's a talent to have to control the sale, to break through buying beliefs. They know how to do it. They're doing it unconsciously. They're running an unconscious strategy. They're running a straight line without knowing it. That's all the straight line is. It's how you sell. It's how you sell. It's the magic to it. Great sales people do it automatically. Poor salesmen just don't know. No one showed them. They're not going to say, listen, here's what you got to do. They've read all the books and all this sort of stuff, but no one showed them a strategy that makes it the same every single time. So no matter what their combination is, you're going to run the same patterns in the same order and eventually you're going to hit on it. Now when do you hit on it? Watch. It's so elegant. It's great. It makes me feel like I'm so proud of it. I love it. I get excited, right? It's so elegant because here's what happens. If this is the open and this is the close, okay? Some people you're going to hit their combination earlier and they'll close here. Other people you're going to hit it later in another language pattern and they'll close here. But either way, you're always moving down the line. If you move all the way down the line, either two things, either they buy or they don't buy, right? But what happens is this. You've ran every possible combination to pick the lock. You close everyone who's closeable. And why didn't they buy? Remember what I said in the beginning? No matter how good you are, not everybody's a buyer. That's it. It's as simple as that. But by using the straight line, you're going to know everything you pick up on, I got it. Either he buys from me or he would never buy from anybody. That's a good place to be in when you pick up the phone. Not like a hope I could figure out what to say. Well, hope he doesn't give me that one objection I hate about his mother. Hope he doesn't bring up his wife. Ooh, the wife. I hate the wife objection. That's the worst one. Oh, let me think about it. It's just around the corner, okay? Here's what I say, okay? Tell me that you don't like my product and I'll argue with you to the death about it. I'll argue, logically I'll argue with you to the death about my promise. I know I'm right, right? Tell me you don't like me and I'll try to convince you to like me and say, what did I do wrong? I'll figure it out. We'll talk about that. Tell me you don't like my company. I'll defend my company and resell you on my company. Try to make you see the way. Tell me all those stuff and I will respect you. Just don't tell me you want to think about it because that's crap. That's the one that's crap. Don't just tell me, I don't know, let me think about it. That's why I don't respect. Now, people say it all the time, yeah, great. You can say, I don't respect you. How dare you? No, you don't say that. What I'm saying is, is that at the end of the day, if someone just says to you, listen, I don't like your product, I respect the fact that you didn't buy, I get it. I respect that. I can respect that. Or, honestly, you're not my cup of tea, whatever it is, fine. I learned from my mistakes. But if they just say, I don't know, let me think, what doesn't say anything, what it means is very simply this. You have not, A, made an airtight logical case to buy now. You have not made an emotional case to buy now. It's either one of those two, or you have not cracked through a belief about buying. That's what it is. And what's happening is very simple. Visually, the scales, if they're saying, let me think about it, what does it mean? The negatives are heavier than the positives. They've played the movie out. They say that if they take action right now, there will be more pain and pleasure, so they don't buy. It's as simple as that. You move someone down the line, and you're knocking off negatives, and as you do the scale gets lighter, you're adding on positives. And you know, you crack a belief in the gravity changes, and it's over, and the scale pops up. So don't tell me, like, I just want, what's holding you back? Tell me, honestly, I'll respect it. Just don't tell me I got it, you know. And by the way, some people have to speak to their wife. Some people do. Some people do, but most don't. When someone tells you they want to speak to their wife or their husband, what they're saying is, I'm not 100% sure this is right, and I don't want to be thought a fool. We'll be slapped around, we'll be made fun of, and doing something stupid. Because let me tell you something. If they really just imagine this, okay, ladies, if you, just in the crowd, okay, let's just say that your husband did something, you know, he usually doesn't do stuff on his own, he went out and bought something, okay, and it turned out that it was actually unbelievably effective, and you loved it, and it was great, amazing, and it changed your lives, and it was great. Would you really be pissed at him if it was just, ended up being so, so amazing? Maybe he'd be a little bit, you shouldn't have done that, in the end it would work out okay, yes or no. It would, right, your old knife in your head, okay? If he came home, if he did that, and bought something bad, and then wasted your money, you'd be you fool, you'd be berating the guy. When a guy says, let me speak to my wife, what he's saying is I'm not 100% sure that this is right, this is 100% guaranteed. Because if it was 100% guaranteed, and I don't say this in a vacuum, remember, nothing's in a vacuum, there are, some people have relationships where they have to speak to their spouse. We'll go through that tomorrow, how to get, how to handle that, and get the spouse on the line, get the spouse invested in the process too. We'll get to that, but I'm telling you that 75 or 80% of the time, they don't. They're just not convinced. It's a smokescreen, it's a stall, that's what it is. It means that you haven't knocked out a belief, or mostly, mostly, you haven't created a logical case about why they should buy. They don't think your product is absolutely unbelievable, there's still something in their head that's saying, I don't know, let me think about it, and maybe I need to research more. Watch this, okay? I want to show you something. The three tens that call, write this down, the three tens. Your product, your company, and you, right? You need to get them up to a 10, to a 10, and to a 10. All three. When I say 10, there's three words you have to add on to that. 10 in the moment. 10 in the moment. I get you to do a 10, I run a pattern, a language pattern, and I say, you're so, you know, about your product, and I'm done with it, and it's a great pattern. It's like so compelling that if I say, you see what I'm saying? You like it, like, oh yeah, I know, I see it. Loan mods are the, oh my God, credit or pay, yeah, it's great. And I get them to that point, like, oh my God, yeah. If I say to someone, yeah, does the idea make sense to you? They say, yeah, is that good enough? No. The biggest problem, sales and mistakes, they say, well listen, do you like the program? They say, yeah, it's a good program, and they try to move forward and close. No. Unless that person admits to you, logically, not based on hype, you laid out an airtight, logical case, and the person agrees, oh my God, yeah, I see, this is great. Yes, this is a great product, it fills my needs. You cannot move forward on the line unless that's established. You can't just blow past and say, well, he kind of likes, let me try to close on something else. You can't pick the lock. The first tumbler, you don't start from the last number first, is the first tumbler is logic, and he loves your product. He can't just kind of like it. He's got to love it. So you get someone to attend, you run a pattern, he says, oh my God, no, it's great, yeah, I love it. And let's say he's at a 9.5 or close to a 10. So now you go on to your next pattern, which is going to be about you selling yourself, right? And you start doing that, where is he on the product? He's like, eh, his belief is every second, his belief starts to wane. So what happens is when you run a pattern on somebody, you get into a 10, it's in the moment, in the second, and the second later they start to go down. Buyers remorse, they lose belief in what you're saying. That's how humans are. Take this for granted, no problem, you just know that before I close, I got to do one last notch up back to a 10. You get that? So you get into a 10 in the moment, with the first, with the pattern, you move forward, you get into a 10 for you, you get into a 10 for your company, all three things have been established before you ask for the order. It's got to be reinforce, reinforce, ask for the order. Reinforce, reinforce, ask for the order. You bring up to a 10 again in the moment, a 10 again in the moment, and bam, you let all three line up, and you ask for the order. Calmly, not like ask for the order, ask for the order. You're calmly and elegantly with compassion and a tone that says that you care. That's a pattern, a closing pattern. Now, usually they don't close in the first pattern, but what's happening is a box is forming. It's a logic box and an emotion box, and what happens is they start saying, well, you know, wow, too bad I really wish I wasn't told when I was five that might not to buy from sales, I'd love to buy from this one. He's sharp, he's a tact enthusiast, he seems so honest, he's trustworthy. Wow, what a great product he has. Too bad I can't buy, I don't want to be thought of as being full. That's a belief holding that. He's not saying that, but trust me, it's happening at a deep unconscious level. Or else, he'd be buying. Think about it. Why if someone says they love your product, they love and trust you, they love and trust your company, and they're still not buying it, why? Something's holding them back. It's either money or a belief. And talking to a wife is a belief. They have a belief in their heart. They need to talk to someone else to say yes. It's a belief. So what happens now, this is now earlier on, this is in the straight line, you're now in the closing part, that's your first pattern. Your next pattern is going to run to his daughter, his beliefs, his unbusting beliefs, making him play movies out in his head without him really knowing it. Not to manipulate again, like caution you please, ethics, ethics, ethics, ethics, ethics. Only get people to buy who should be buying, help them, motivate them to take action when they should. Please, use this to empower people to make good decisions. Don't manipulate them. But what's going to happen is you're going to run a pattern that's going to cause them to play out movies and they're going to realize that their belief is crazy. They're not even a real, you're going to create scenarios where the downside is played out, it's minimal, the upside's huge, they see themselves being loved and revered by all. And then what do you do? Do you ask them to let them know? You run the three tens again, ten, ten, ten, boom. Ask for the order. Three turns to the right, now another combinator, you get it? Now that might have been one belief you knocked out, but maybe it's another. So what do you do? Very simple. You'll loop back again. Okay, you're in a different pattern. You keep running different patterns and knocking out different beliefs and adding on different positives, different positives, knocking out more negatives. You don't know everyone's buying strategy. You can't be that smart. No one's that intuitive. We know basic stuff. I'll show you stuff about kinesthetic people, visual people, auditory. People process different. I'll show you that, but don't fall back on that. You just to know it can cut your time, but when you get good and elegant with this thing. But for right now, just say, I can't figure out what's going on. Some guys might exactly, I just know, but hit everything logically and close the guy. Now, when you're in this latter portions of the line, what are you focusing on? Guess what? It's based on the intelligence that you gathered here. All of a sudden, all those questions that you asked him in the perfect tone out, which made him know that you like him, that you care about him, that you're honest and ethical. All that stuff now is in your arsenal. And you start running patterns specifically geared to his strengths that you know he loves about your product. Always looking to accomplish any weaknesses that might have been uncovered from that strategy. And you get from the intelligence gathering. You start knocking that stuff very specifically. You start broad and get specific. Broad and get specific as you go down the line, your razor honed into the sky. You're in rapport. You have all the intelligence you know to close. And you're hitting out as negative in a laser-guided fashion in the last portion of sale. And if this way, are they going to buy or they're not? But what you're going to know is that you closed everybody who was closeable with this strategy. Everyone who's closeable will close. Just don't close the ones that shouldn't buy. This strategy, this system, it literally will change the way you go about closing people. It will empower you to become a huge producer, whatever you sell. Anything. I've trained all sorts of companies to sell things. It could be for sales that occur over three months. They require a million dollar deals, investment backing deals, like go on and on, big deals. All this sort of stuff, all is the straight line. It's the same thing. Use this technology in an honest, ethical way and you will soar. You will fly. You will flourish. Because you have the keys. Who you are, who your company is and what your product does. And they're all great for the person. You know what their beliefs are and you need to knock them out. So I want you to remember that the straight line technique is a game of percentages. You're going to close everybody who's closeable, but that doesn't mean you're going to close everybody. What you have here though is a snowball effect. That as you start to master these techniques one by one, they will slowly come together. When I say slowly, I don't mean over years, I'm talking about weeks. That's it. A month on the outside, but they'll come together and you will see a dramatic improvement in your ability to close, to persuade, to really make your visions known to another human being. And remember this here, one of the most important things with the straight line technique is that you have to get to the point of being unconsciously competent. Now there's also a good enough factor, which means even if you're not unconsciously competent, you'll still get results. But it's the difference between getting to a 10 out of 10 with the straight line where you literally can close everyone who's closeable, versus being a 5 or a 6 out of 10, where you'll see a 30 or 40% increase in your sales and your ability to persuade. But that sort of real power, it comes at the very top of the game. To get there, the action steps are as follows. Number one, I want you to practice visualization. I want you to actually see yourself in the future using the straight line. I want you to see yourself closing sales. It's called future pacing, as I said. And future pacing means you're actually going to run this movie out and you're going to see the benefits. You're going to see yourself in a disassociated state, meaning you'll see yourself in the picture and then you'll associate into the picture. And you'll allow the wonderful feelings to literally wash over you to enjoy in the fruits of your labor of mastering the straight line and seeing just how powerful it is in the future and enjoying the fruits of that. So again, visualize, I want you to do it every day in the morning once, 10 minutes at night time, 10 minutes, and then you get the 15 that's pretty good too. But at least give yourself 10 minutes and allow yourself the pleasure of seeing you in the picture using the straight line and having amazing results with it, which is exactly what you're going to have.
